lifecycle_stages.name: Waste generation
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Households, businesses, industry, and construction sites produce waste.

lifecycle_stages.name: Source separation
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Sorting at the point of origin (currently mostly absent in Pristina).

lifecycle_stages.name: Collection
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Pastrimi trucks (and informal collectors) gather waste from bins.

lifecycle_stages.name: Transport
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Movement from collection points to landfill or treatment facilities.

lifecycle_stages.name: Sorting / MRF / treatment
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Materials Recovery Facilities — none currently exist in Kosovo.

lifecycle_stages.name: Recovery & EPR / DRS
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Recycling, deposit-refund, EPR producer responsibility schemes.

lifecycle_stages.name: Billing & financing
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Tariffs, tipping fees, EU funding, donor grants, landfill tax.

lifecycle_stages.name: Regulation & policy
lifecycle_stages.blurb: Strategy, laws, EU acquis, ministerial decisions.

actors.name: Ministry of Environment, Spatial Planning and Infrastructure

rich.label: Ministry of Env.
rich.fullName: Ministry of Environment, Spatial Planning and Infrastructure (MMPHI)
rich.role: National ministry responsible for environmental policy and waste. Formally approved the Dec 2024 regulation as "in line with the Law on Waste." Holds licensing authority over operators — has not used it to sanction for service failures.
leverage: Operating license authority over all waste operators
Drafts national waste strategy and laws
MMPHI approval blocked Municipality's legal challenge to the Dec 2024 regulation
dependencies: Municipal compliance for ground-level enforcement
EU accession pressure to actually reform
Budget and staffing for enforcement capacity
fears: EU accession review finding systemic non-compliance
Mirash environmental disaster triggering liability

stats.label: Enforcement actions
stats.value: Near zero on operator performance failures

rich.label: Env. Agency (AMMK)
rich.role: Environmental monitoring agency. Maintains the official illegal landfill register. Confirmed no Kosovo sanitary landfill has an integrated environmental permit. Issues reports. Does not enforce.
leverage: Official data source on illegal landfills
Confirmed environmental non-compliance at Mirash
dependencies: MMPHI for enforcement authority
Municipal cooperation for ground-level monitoring

actors.name: Ministry of Industry, Entrepreneurship and Trade
National Institute of Public Health of Kosovo

rich.label: Ombudsperson
rich.role: Hilmi Jashari. Investigated the May 2025 waste crisis. Convened mediation meeting — Pastrimi's CEO attended, Mayor Rama did not. Has long history of environmental rights advocacy, including warnings about Mirash methane risk.
leverage: Public moral authority and press attention
Can convene parties and issue public recommendations
Long track record on Mirash environmental warnings (since 2017)
dependencies: Parties willing to engage (Mayor Rama declined in May 2025)
No enforcement authority — can only recommend and publicize
fears: Being ignored — which happened publicly in May 2025

stats.label: Mirash warning (2017)
stats.value: "A permanent danger which could cause the death of people"

stats.label: On courts
stats.value: "The prosecution and courts are not giving priority to environmental cases"

rich.quote: "Complete irresponsibility of the representatives of the municipality in relation to the interests and concerns of the citizens of Pristina."

rich.label: Municipality
rich.role: Capital city local government. Main shareholder of Pastrimi. Lost billing authority via Dec 2024 regulation. Issued competing bills Jan–Jun 2025. Hired private operators during strike. Forced to return €2.7M to Pastrimi after June 2025 Supreme Court ruling.
leverage: Main shareholder of Pastrimi
Controls client registry — can withhold registrations
Can declare emergencies and contract private operators
Won local election late 2025 — political mandate
dependencies: Municipal Assembly cooperation (which it lacks — PDK+VV majority)
Pastrimi actually collecting waste
Court system not upholding opposition-passed regulations
fears: Pastrimi bankruptcy under LDK watch
Being blamed for service failures caused by the billing dispute
Private operator tender being permanently blocked
rich.quote: "I apologize to the citizens who are facing confusion because it is true that 'Pastrimi', citing the changes to the regulation made by the municipal assembly, has started collecting invoices for 2025."
rich.quoteSource: Bekim Brestovci (Municipality Director of Public Services), KOHA January 2025

rich.label: Municipal Assembly
rich.role: Legislative body of the municipality. PDK and VV hold working majority. Passed the December 5, 2024 regulation granting Pastrimi exclusive billing authority — against Mayor Rama's objection. The triggering instrument of the 2024–2025 crisis.
leverage: Legislative power to pass municipal regulations
Dec 2024 regulation upheld by both MMPHI and Supreme Court
Can block municipal executive agenda
dependencies: PDK+VV coalition holding together
National-level political dynamics not overriding local coalition
fears: National VV leadership overriding local VV Assembly behavior
LDK winning enough seats to reverse the regulation

stats.label: MMPHI response
stats.value: Approved as "in line with the Law on Waste"

rich.label: Pastrimi
rich.role: Municipal waste collection company. ~800–900 employees. Exclusive collection territory in Pristina under Dec 2024 regulation, upheld June 2025. Self-financing: covers all costs from citizen fees. CEO Petrit Reçica.
leverage: Supreme Court-backed exclusive billing authority
Exclusive collection territory — no legal competitor currently
800–900 unionized workers who can strike
Ability to threaten bankruptcy (and the public health crisis it implies)
dependencies: Municipality must register clients for billing
KLMC must accept trucks at Mirash
Citizen payment compliance
Trucks operational (JICA grant covers 10 new)
fears: Municipality withholding client registry (23,000-client gap = ~€3.5M/year lost)
KLMC refusing trucks over €1.9M unpaid debt
Private operators entering territory
Revenue too low to cover operating costs
rich.quote: "From January 2025 onwards, it is our sole competence."

actors.name: Private Waste Operators (emergency contracts)
actors.note: Hired by KOM during Feb-Jun 2025 emergency

rich.label: Private Operators
rich.role: Five private operators that the Municipality attempted to contract via a €16M tender in 2024. Blocked by the Dec 2024 regulation. Temporarily hired during the May 2025 strike — "they also failed to do the job properly." Currently blocked from Pristina territory.
leverage: Municipality political backing if LDK wins Assembly majority
EU preference for private-sector waste management
dependencies: Municipality successfully overturning the Dec 2024 regulation
Political shift in Municipal Assembly composition

stats.label: Emergency use
stats.value: Contracted during May 2025 strike — poor performance

rich.label: KLMC
rich.role: Publicly owned company operating Kosovo's public landfills, including Mirash. Reports to Ministry of Economy. Pastrimi owes KLMC ~€1.9M in unpaid landfill fees. Mirash has exceeded capacity for years with no leachate treatment and no environmental permit.
leverage: Can refuse Pastrimi trucks at Mirash — which would stop collection city-wide
Only viable disposal site for Pristina-region waste
dependencies: Pastrimi payments for operating revenue
MMPHI for policy direction and funding
EU for closure/replacement funding
fears: Pastrimi debt growing while KLMC cannot refuse service
Mirash environmental failure triggering liability
EU-mandated Mirash closure before replacement site is ready
rich.quote: "The problem with sewage at this landfill has been out of control for many years because the collection point has flooded."

actors.name: Mirash Landfill
actors.note: Operating beyond capacity, EU flagged for closure ~10 years ago

actors.name: Illegal Dumpsites (458 official / ~1,572 civil society count)

rich.label: Citizens
rich.role: ~95,000 registered Pastrimi clients (disputed: Municipality count is 72,000). Pay €6.20/month flat fee. During 2025 crisis, ~21,000 paid the Municipality instead of Pastrimi, creating a dual-billing situation. No pay-as-you-throw mechanism.
leverage: Collective non-payment can destabilize Pastrimi financially
Complaints and press attention create political costs for both sides
dependencies: Pastrimi for collection — no alternative for household waste
Clarity on where to pay (which broke down in 2025)
fears: Double billing
Non-collection and associated health/smell problems
Paying for a service that doesn't arrive
rich.quote: "The waste in every corner is causing us problems even in business. The containers are of poor quality and insufficient given the high volume."

actors.name: Industry / Construction sector
actors.note: Largest single source of illegal CDW dumping

actors.name: EU Office in Pristina / EU Delegation

rich.label: EU Delegation
rich.role: Annual progress reports flag waste management failures. Funded all 7 Kosovo public landfills (2001–2008). Leverage via EU accession conditionality — rarely deployed on this issue specifically.
leverage: EU accession conditionality for Kosovo
Annual progress reports with public impact
Original funder of Kosovo landfill infrastructure
dependencies: Kosovo political will to implement reforms
MMPHI to act on policy recommendations
fears: Kosovo waste situation becoming a diplomatic liability
Reversal of EU-funded landfill investment

stats.label: Conditionality status
stats.value: Advisory — no binding deadline on waste

rich.quote: "Only a few landfills require entrance fees, which only cover the basic cost of landfill management. For waste collection, there is no pay-as-you-throw system."
rich.quoteSource: EU Office in Pristina, Balkan Insight 2025

actors.name: Press (KOHA, Telegrafi, Prishtina Insight, Balkan Insight, RFE/RL)
Hybrid Regulator
actors.note: NEW - independent regulator for tariff setting, performance monitoring, dispute resolution. Analogous to ARRU (water) / ERO (energy).

actors.name: Cross-Sector Coordination Body
actors.note: NEW - coordinates policies + investments across central + local + private + civil society + donors. INDEP/KAS Recommendation 2 named.

actors.name: Waste Management Zone 1
actors.note: NEW - one of five inter-municipal cooperation zones legally enshrined

actors.name: National Waste Information System
actors.note: NEW - mandatory reporting by municipalities, operators, PROs

actors.name: PRO - Packaging
actors.note: Producer Responsibility Org for packaging EPR

actors.name: Performance-Based Municipal Financing
actors.note: INDEP/KAS Recommendation 5; ties transfers to recycling outcomes

actors.name: Law on Integrated Waste Management (replaces 04/L-060)
actors.note: Government-approved Feb-Mar 2026; Assembly approval expected Aug-Sep 2026

actors.name: EPR for WEEE / Batteries / ELV / Tyres
Full Net Cost Coverage principle (FNCC)
actors.note: No taxpayer subsidy of waste services; legally locks PAYT + EPR cost recovery

activities.name: Pass municipal waste regulation

MoEcon.note: approves as "in line with Law on Waste"

activities.note: The Dec 2024 Regulation No. 01-030/01-161704/24 was passed by ASMB (PDK + VV bloc) over Mayor Rama's (LDK) objection. KOM as executive opposes; ASMB is the A,R. MMPHI formally signed off on the amendment as legally compliant — that's an "I (approves)" role.

activities.name: Set tariff (€6.20/mo flat, no PAYT)
activities.note: Flat €6.20/mo with VAT. Pastrimi sets the rate; the Assembly authorizes. Both claim authority; KOM disputes it post-Dec 2024. ⚠ flag captures the live dispute. Note: under the new law, FNCC + Hybrid Regulator (HReg) take over tariff-setting accountability — see `policy.md` row 18.

activities.name: Maintain client registry
activities.note: The flashpoint. Pastrimi count: ~95k clients. KOM count: ~72k. Pastrimi alleges KOM has refused to register ~25k new clients since 2023, costing ~€3.5M/year. Both ⚠ — neither side is functionally executing this.

activities.name: Bill citizens for waste services
activities.note: Status changed mid-2025. Before June: chaotic — both sides billed, ~21k citizens paid Komuna instead of Pastrimi by April. After Supreme Court ruling June 2025: Pastrimi has clean R; KOM forced to return collected funds via mediation June 25. Private operators billed during Feb-Jun 2025 emergency window — ⚠.

activities.name: Pay landfill tipping fees
activities.note: Pastrimi owes KLMC ~€1.9M. Both sides have leverage (KLMC could refuse trucks; Pastrimi pays KLMC's revenue) — mutual hostage situation. ⚠.

activities.name: Appoint Pastrimi Board (5-7)
activities.note: Whiteboard finding: Pastrimi CEO is appointed by a 5-7 person Board, which is appointed by the State (specifically Min of Economy as the operator's parent ministry). This means **the shareholder (Komuna) has no operational control over the company they "own."** Major structural finding.

activities.name: Resolve regulation disputes
activities.note: Supreme Court ruled in Pastrimi's favor June 2025 (R). Constitutional Court referral happened but ruling status unclear in available sources — flagged as ? for follow-up. Under the new law, in-sector disputes route to the Hybrid Regulator (post-2027) — see `policy.md` row 21.

activities.name: (new-law) Operate Civic Amenity Centers (CACs)
activities.note: New facilities for bulky/special waste drop-off referenced by the Mazreku deck (slide 20) as part of the household-facing source-separation architecture. Per the GIZ MPG-CE / CE-PG pitch, CACs are explicitly listed as an infrastructure type to be built under the proposed €20M CE-PG (2024-2027) and would be **shared zonally** rather than built per-municipality. KOM operates within its perimeter; WMZ operates the cross-municipal shared-CAC layer; KLMC consulted on integration with regional infrastructure; citizens are R as users (drop-off behavior is the whole point of the facility).

activities.name: (new-law) Operate Materials Recovery Facilities (MRFs) — none currently exist
activities.rawName: **(new-law)** Operate Materials Recovery Facilities (MRFs) — *none currently exist*
activities.note: Sorting plants for separated streams. **None currently exist in Kosovo** — confirmed by GIZ deck and DYVÓ. Listed in the CE-PG infrastructure menu. KLMC is the natural A,R as the existing public landfill/treatment operator with regional scope; WMZ operates zonal MRFs as part of the shared backbone; Pastrimi is C as the upstream collection operator delivering material; MMPHI informed via NWIS reporting. The structural absence today is exactly why the row is included — the matrix should make the gap visible.

activities.name: (new-law) Coordinate within Five Waste Management Zones
activities.rawName: **(new-law)** Coordinate within Five Waste Management Zones
activities.note: WMZ as the new structural actor is A,R for cross-municipal operational coordination (joint procurement, shared infrastructure, zonal planning). KOM is R for participating (joining the zone they are assigned to per the legal designation). MMPHI consulted on policy framework. This row is the operational complement to `policy.md` row 20 (where MMPHI is A,R for *establishing* the zones).

activities.name: Handle cross-neighborhood "bin tourism"
activities.note: Documented by DYVÓ across multiple municipalities — Ferizaj respondents specifically reported residents from other neighborhoods crossing in to dump in their bins. **No clean A exists today.** KOM is marked ⚠ as the phantom A — accountability is implicitly with the municipality but functionally nobody enforces it. INSP is R *when* fines are applied, but DYVÓ found citizens "almost none had been fined for waste violations" and "did not know where to report illegal waste." Citizens are I (affected, not in the loop). The row is included to make the enforcement gap legible — a finding from DYVÓ that the existing matrix didn't capture.

activities.name: Respond to climate × waste events (e.g. Mitrovica Lushta River flooding Jan 2023)
activities.rawName: Respond to climate × waste events (e.g. Mitrovica Lushta River flooding Jan 2023)

activities.name: Set National Waste Strategy 2024-2030
activities.note: The 2024-2030 strategy was adopted by MMPHI in April 2024 (revising the original 2021-2030 strategy). MMPHI is the clean A,R. AMMK supplies data; MINT, MoFin, MoAg consulted; municipalities and operators are I (informed of national targets but not part of drafting).

activities.name: Allocate Action Plan budget (€41M / €77M)
activities.note: Action Plan 2024-2026 = €41M; total CE program 2024-2030 = €77M. Allocation runs through MoFin; the EU's Reform & Growth Facility (€6B 2024-2027) is the largest Consulted source for the Western Balkans bucket. Donors are also C (GIZ, JICA, World Bank).

activities.name: Design DRS for beverage packaging
activities.note: Secondary legislation passed 2024. MMPHI is A,R for the design. PROs (Producer Responsibility Organizations) are listed as the eventual R for operation, but no PROs are publicly known to be operational yet — the ? on row 4 captures this.

activities.name: Operate DRS (post-launch)
activities.note:
Planned launch January 2025. Verification issue #4 open. The "R ?" on PRO is the document's plan; reality may be no operator yet, or MMPHI directly operating.

**Rows 5-6 — EPR systems.** EPR is the conceptual frame for packaging + WEEE/batteries. MMPHI owns the policy A; PROs are the planned R but don't yet exist as known operators. Industry (producers) are Consulted because EPR shifts cost onto them.

activities.name: Mandate source separation (households + bio)
activities.note: MMPHI sets the rule; municipalities operate; collection operators (Pastrimi etc.) actually do the separated collection. Multiple R is correct here — separation requires action at all three levels.

activities.name: Set CDW recovery targets (80% policy)
activities.note: Kosovo policy says 80% target. Construction sector is the R (must comply at site). Implementation is reportedly limited — the dossier flags CDW as the largest single illegal-dumping driver.

activities.name: Implement landfill tax
activities.note: Recommendation 10 from INDEP/KAS. Doesn't exist yet in Kosovo. If implemented, MoFin would be A,R via legislative amendment.

activities.name: Run Green Public Procurement
activities.note: Procurement Regulatory Commission (under MoFin) is A,R. Recommendation 12 from INDEP/KAS. Currently weak.

activities.name: EU acquis alignment + progress reporting
activities.note: Two A's because there are two distinct accountabilities: MMPHI for *implementing* the acquis, EU for *assessing* compliance. Acceptable double-A here.

activities.name: Inter-ministerial CE coordination unit
activities.note: Recommendation 2 from INDEP/KAS. Doesn't exist yet. MMPHI as proposed A.

activities.name: National data system for material flows
activities.rawName: National data system for material flows
activities.note: Recommendation 1 from INDEP/KAS. AMMK is the operational R (it already runs environmental databases); MMPHI accountable for the policy; data quality is widely flagged as fragmented. Note: the new-law NWIS (row 19) is the post-2027 successor / formalization of this system, with mandatory reporting from KOM, WMZ, operators, and PROs.

activities.name: (new-law) Develop new Law on Integrated Waste Management
activities.rawName: **(new-law)** Develop new Law on Integrated Waste Management
activities.note: MMPHI is A and R for drafting, per the Mazreku/MMPHI deck. EU is C on acquis-alignment review. Government approval (CG = Council of Gov, R for the approval-phase decision) targeted for Feb-Mar 2026. Stakeholders consulted across drafting cycle. HReg does not yet exist so does not participate in its own creation.

activities.name: (new-law) Approve new Law on Integrated Waste Management
activities.rawName: **(new-law)** Approve new Law on Integrated Waste Management
activities.note: The Assembly (ASMB) is the formal R for adoption (Aug-Sep 2026 target window). MMPHI / Government is A as sponsor. All other actors are I — they are informed of the legislative outcome but do not participate in the vote.

activities.name: (new-law) Set tariffs under Full Net Cost Coverage (FNCC)
activities.rawName: **(new-law)** Set tariffs under Full Net Cost Coverage (FNCC)
activities.note: Full Net Cost Coverage (FNCC) is the new principle that waste tariffs must recover the full net system cost (not subsidized below cost). Hybrid Regulator (HReg) is A,R for setting tariffs under this principle. MMPHI consulted on policy framework, MoFin consulted on fiscal interaction. KOM and WMZ are I (must apply tariffs); operators and PROs are I (must collect them).

activities.name: (new-law) Operate National Waste Information System (NWIS)
activities.note: MMPHI accountable; AMMK operational R (continues its data role from row 15). New under the law: mandatory reporting from KOM, WMZ, operators, and PROs — these are all R because the regime depends on each of them feeding clean data in. HReg is C (uses NWIS data for tariff and performance functions). This is the formalization + expansion of row 15.

activities.name: (new-law) Establish Five Waste Management Zones
activities.note: MMPHI A,R for establishing the zones (legal designation, perimeter, governance template). Municipalities are R for participating (joining the zone they are assigned to). WMZ as the new structural actor is R for operating the zonal infrastructure once stood up. HReg is C (will regulate zonal tariffs and performance under FNCC).

activities.name: Issue operating licenses to waste operators
activities.rawName: Issue operating licenses to waste operators
activities.note: MMPHI has clean A,R. Notably, **MMPHI has not used licensing as a sanction mechanism** even when Pastrimi service failures were severe. The authority exists; the muscle doesn't.

activities.name: Inspect Pastrimi/operators for service compliance
activities.note: MMPHI is A; municipal inspectors are R. But: municipal inspectors are politically constrained from fining the public enterprise their Municipality owns. The A and R are misaligned in incentive — inspectors work for KOM, KOM is Pastrimi's shareholder.

activities.name: Inspect landfills for environmental compliance
activities.note: AMMK is the operational R (environmental monitoring). They've publicly stated Kosovo landfills lack leachate treatment, integrated environmental permits, daily cover compliance. **They report; they don't enforce.** No A is exercised.

activities.name: Fine illegal dumpers (households)
activities.note: Komuna A, municipal inspectors R. Police can also issue fines (C). Per Atlas Institute: enforcement is "sporadic," targets visible offenders, rarely households.

activities.name: Fine illegal dumpers (construction sites)
activities.note: Same authority structure as row 4. **In practice, near-zero enforcement** even though construction is the documented largest source of illegal dumping. Pastrimi has reported names + addresses to authorities; no further action taken (per Springer 2023 study).

activities.name: Sanction operators for service failures
activities.note: MMPHI A. Tools available include license revocation, regulatory sanctions. **Not used against Pastrimi during the 2024-25 crisis** despite documented service failures. Phantom A.

activities.name: Maintain illegal-dumpsite register
activities.note: AMMK official count: 458 (2024), 403 (2023). Civil society + joint AMMK/GIZ studies: 1,572 (2021), 500+ (Let's Do It Kosovo current). The methodological gap is informative — different counts serve different purposes.

activities.name: Maintain national waste-flow data
activities.note: AMMK runs the system. INDEP/KAS Recommendation 1 calls for a new national material-flow database — i.e., the current system is inadequate. Row 15 (NWIS) is the post-2027 successor / formalization with mandatory reporting.

activities.name: Audit EPR/DRS compliance (when operational)
activities.note: Conditional on those systems operating. Currently ? in practice (issues #4, #5). This is the *current-state* row; row 17 is the post-new-law version where HReg becomes A,R.

activities.name: Adjudicate waste-related disputes
activities.note: Courts are clean A,R. Supreme Court ruled June 2025 in Pastrimi's favor on the regulation dispute. Constitutional Court referral status unclear. Note: post-2027, in-sector tariff and performance disputes route to HReg (see policy.md row 21); courts remain backup for criminal and constitutional matters.

activities.name: Investigate environmental health risks
activities.note: NIPHK/IKSHPK has authority but rarely activates. The Feb 2025 emergency was politically declared, not NIPHK-driven.

activities.name: Receive citizen reports of illegal dumping (current channels)
activities.rawName: Receive citizen reports of illegal dumping (current channels)
activities.note: Pre-existing row, retained. Captures the *current* analog/phone-based reporting paths through KOM and INSP. The DYVÓ citizen survey (n=150) found citizens **did not know where to report illegal waste** — some guessed police, some guessed inspection services, **none had ever reported illegal dumping**. The gap that the Trash Reporter app (HackTheTrash) is filling. **See row 16** for the structured-digital-reports extension and **row 15** for how NWIS could give those reports a formal data home.

activities.name: Public watchdog — independent counts/critique
activities.note: Civil society is doing the work that AMMK should be doing. Let's Do It Kosovo, INDEP, Atlas Institute, BIRN-affiliated outlets all publish independent counts and critiques.

activities.name: Press accountability journalism
activities.note: KOHA, Telegrafi, Prishtina Insight, Balkan Insight, RFE/RL all cover the waste beat. They're the de facto enforcement of accountability when formal channels don't act.

activities.name: (new-law) Operate the National Waste Information System (NWIS)
activities.rawName: **(new-law)** Operate the National Waste Information System (NWIS)
activities.note: Per Mazreku/MMPHI deck §7 (slide 11), the new law establishes a National Waste Information System with **mandatory reporting** by municipalities, operators, and PROs, plus strengthened inspection powers and regular performance/compliance audits. MMPHI is A (policy ownership); AMMK is operational R (continues data role from row 8); KOM, operators, and PROs are all R as mandatory reporters — the regime depends on each feeding clean data in. HReg is C (uses NWIS data for tariff and performance functions). Citizens are I in this row; row 16 is where citizen input flows into the architecture if a structured channel is built.

activities.name: Receive structured citizen reports (digital) ⚠
activities.note: New row. The DYVÓ citizen research surfaced an explicit gap: citizens have no formal channel for digital, structured, evidence-bearing reports. Currently NO clean A in formal channels — KOM and INSP are marked ⚠ to flag that authority *should* live there but does not in practice. The Trash Reporter app (HackTheTrash) is positioned as the proposed R for intake. Per the Mazreku/MMPHI deck §11, the Reporter app could be designed as **a citizen-facing front-end to NWIS** (row 15), turning citizen reports into a structured input to state data infrastructure rather than a parallel system. *Cross-reference any open Reporter-app verification issue when filed.*

activities.name: (new-law) Audit EPR/DRS compliance (post-new-law operational regime)
activities.note: Post-new-law operational regime. Conditional on EPR and DRS systems operating (verification issues #4, #5). HReg is A,R for performance non-compliance audit; MMPHI is C on policy framework; PROs and operators are R as mandatory reporters subject to audit. This is the post-2027 successor to row 9 and shifts audit accountability from MMPHI's "phantom A" to a dedicated regulator — *if* HReg stands up with capacity.

activities.name: Investigate cross-municipal waste flows ("bin tourism")
activities.note: Emerges from the dossier's system-map data plus DYVÓ's "bin tourism" finding (cross-neighborhood + cross-municipal waste movement that GPS evidence could expose). MMPHI is A as policy owner; AMMK is R for the data side; KOM is C (each municipality owns part of the picture). WMZ-level cooperation is needed but not yet operational — the new law's Five Waste Management Zones will eventually be the structural answer here, but until 2027 there is no operational R for cross-jurisdiction flow investigation.

activities.name: National data system for material flows
activities.rawName: National data system for material flows
activities.note: Foundational; everything else depends on it. AMMK already runs environmental databases — they're the operational R. The recommendation is to expand into material flows specifically. Currently rated as fragmented data systems by INDEP/KAS itself.

activities.name: Inter-Ministerial CE Coordination Unit
activities.note: Doesn't exist. Office of the Prime Minister (CG) would be the establishment R via government decision; MMPHI is the policy A. **High-leverage card** because so many other recommendations depend on cross-ministerial coordination.

activities.name: EPR for priority streams (packaging, WEEE, batteries, textiles)
activities.rawName: EPR for priority streams (packaging, WEEE, batteries, textiles)
activities.note: PRO column is "R to-form" — the Producer Responsibility Organizations don't exist yet; they're created when EPR is operationalized. Industry/producers are C because EPR shifts cost onto them.

activities.name: DRS national rollout
activities.note: Already has secondary legislation (2024); planned launch January 2025. Status unverified (issue #4). MMPHI is A,R for the system; PROs operate (R ? until verified).

activities.name: Performance-based municipal financing
activities.note: Tie municipal transfers to recycling + landfill diversion outcomes. MoFin is the operational R (controls the transfers); MMPHI is the policy A. Currently doesn't exist — funding is not performance-linked.

activities.name: Mandatory source separation (recyclables + bio)
activities.note: MMPHI sets the rule; municipalities operate; **citizens are the unrecognized R** — separation only works if households actually separate. Citizens listed as R captures this.

activities.name: National investment program: recycling, composting, sorting infrastructure
activities.rawName: National investment program: recycling, composting, sorting infrastructure
activities.note: MoFin allocates; the EU's Reform & Growth Facility (€6B 2024-2027) is the largest funding pool. **Donor R via PPP** (public-private partnership) is the implementation pattern.

activities.name: C&D waste recovery system (80% target)
activities.note: Construction sector is **R (must comply)**. This is the single biggest waste stream by volume; non-compliance is the documented largest illegal-dumping driver. Hard cell to fill — the actor most resistant to the rule is the actor who has to do it.

activities.name: Industrial symbiosis platform
activities.note: MINT is the lead institution per the doc. Industry participates (R). Doesn't exist yet. Trepça lead-battery recycling is the closest existing example (verification issue #5).

activities.name: Landfill tax to incentivize recycling
activities.note: MoFin owns it (legislative amendment + revenue). Doesn't exist yet. UK comparison: ~£126/ton; Ireland ~€80/ton. INDEP/KAS proposes gradual introduction to avoid spiking illegal dumping.

activities.name: Reuse + repair centers in main municipalities
activities.rawName: Reuse + repair centers in main municipalities
activities.note: Municipalities operate; civil society is also R (NGOs run reuse centers in many comparison cities). Citizens are R because the model only works if they actually use the centers.

activities.name: Green public procurement requirements
activities.note: Public Procurement Regulatory Commission (under MoFin) is A,R. Currently weak. Industry is C as supplier base.

activities.name: Strengthen enforcement vs illegal dumping
activities.note: Municipal inspectors are R; MMPHI is A. **Citizens are also R via reporting** — and the Trash Reporter app (HackTheTrash) is exactly the citizen-side tool the recommendation calls for.

activities.name: Financial schemes for CE innovation
activities.note: Donors are R (funding + grants); MoFin is A. The EU's RGF is the biggest pool. PROs not directly involved.
